revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23985667504|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Hare of Inaba]][[ja:因幡の白兎]][[ko:이나바의 흰 토끼]][[pl:Zając z Inaby]][[ru:Белый заяц из Инабы]][[th:กระต่ายป่าแห่งอินาบะ]][[vi:Thỏ Inaba]][[zh:因幡之白兔]]
{{Short description|Japanese myths}} [[Image:Shirousagi -02.jpg|250px|thumb|The Hare of Inaba and [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-kami]] at {{ill|Hakuto Shrine|en|Hakuto Shrine|qid=Q246667|lt=Hakuto Shrine|1=Hakuto Shrine}} in Tottori]] [[Image:Hakutojinjahonden.JPG|thumb|right|250px|[[Honden]] main hall of the Hakuto Shrine, dedicated to the Hare of Inaba]] The {{nihongo|'''Hare of Inaba'''|因幡の白兎|Inaba no Shirousagi}} can refer to two distinct [[Japanese mythology|Japanese myths]], both from the ancient {{ill|Inaba Province|en|Inaba Province|qid=Q1054338|lt=province of Inaba|1=Inaba Province}}, now the eastern part of {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|qid=Q133935|lt=Tottori Prefecture|1=Tottori Prefecture}}. The Hare of Inaba legend belongs to the ''Izumo denrai'', or tradition of myths originating from the {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|qid=Q582846|lt=Izumo region|1=Izumo Province}}.<ref name="kokugo" /><ref name="d" /><ref name="n" /> The Hare of Inaba forms an essential part of the legend of the [[Shinto]] god ''Ōnamuchi-no-kami'', which was the name for [[Ōkuninushi]] within this legend.<ref name="merriam" />
The hare referred to in the legend is the ''Lepus brachyurus'', or {{ill|Japanese hare|en|Japanese hare|qid=Q1355766|lt=Japanese hare|1=Japanese hare}}, possibly the subspecies found on the {{ill|Oki Islands|en|Oki Islands|qid=Q483146|lt=Oki Islands|1=Oki Islands}} known as the ''Lepus brachyurus okiensis''. The Japanese hare ranges between {{Convert|43|cm|in}} and {{Convert|54|cm|in}} in length, and is much smaller than the common {{ill|European hare|en|European hare|qid=Q26838|lt=European hare|1=European hare}}. Japanese hares are typically brown, but may turn white during winter in areas with a varying climate, such as that of the Inaba region.<ref name="nousagi" /><ref name="nousagi-d" />
==Kojiki version== One version of the tale of the Hare of Inaba is found in the ''[[Kojiki]]'', the oldest extant chronicle in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, which dates from early in the 8th century (711-712). The legend appears in the first of the three sections of the ''Kojiki'', the ''Kamitsumaki'', also known as the ''Jindai no Maki'', or "Volume of the Age of the Gods". This section of the ''Kojiki'' outlines the myths concerning the foundation of Japan prior to the birth of the {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|qid=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}, the {{ill|List of emperors of Japan|en|List of emperors of Japan|qid=Q188210|lt=first|1=List of emperors of Japan}} {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}}.<ref name="kokugo" />
In the ''Kojiki'' version of the myth, a {{ill|Hare|en|Hare|qid=Q46076|lt=Hare|1=Hare}} tricks some ''wanizame''{{efn|''Wanizame'' may refer to the {{ill|Wani (dragon)|en|Wani (dragon)|qid=Q20049265|lt=wani|1=Wani (dragon)}}, a {{ill|Japanese dragon|en|Japanese dragon|qid=Q2366503|lt=dragon|1=Japanese dragon}} or {{ill|Sea monster|en|Sea monster|qid=Q920028|lt=Sea monster|1=Sea monster}} in [[Japanese mythology]]. In the {{ill|San'in region|en|San'in region|qid=Q398310|lt=San'in region|1=San'in region}}, from which the myth originates, ''wanizame'' is synonymous with the common shark.<ref name="wanizame-k" /><ref name="wanizame-n" />}} into being used as a land bridge in order to travel from the {{ill|Oki Islands|en|Oki Islands|qid=Q483146|lt=Island of Oki|1=Oki Islands}} to [[Cape Keta]]. Cape Keta is now identified with the Hakuto Coast in the present-day city of [[:en:Tottori, Tottori|Tottori]].<ref name="keta" /><ref name="hakuto" /> The hare challenges the sharks to see whose clan is larger—that of the sharks, or that of the hares.<ref name="n" /> The hare had the sharks lie in a row across the sea. The hare then hopped across them, counting them as he went. Nearing the end, the hare exclaims that he has deceived the sharks in order to use them as a bridge. The last shark attacks the hare, ripping his fur from him.<ref name="d" /><ref name="n" />
Ōnamuchi-no-kami (Ōkuninushi's name at that time) and his eighty brothers were traveling through the Inaba region to woo [[Princess Yakami]] of Inaba.<ref name="kokushi" /> While the brothers were on their way to visit the princess, the flayed hare stopped them and asked them for help. Rather than helping the hare, they advised it to wash in the sea and dry itself in the wind, which naturally caused it great pain.<ref name="kokushi" /> In contrast, Ōnamuchi, unlike his quarreling elder brothers, told the hare to bathe in fresh water from the mouth of a river, and then roll in the pollen of [[:en:Cattail|Cattail]]s.<ref name="d" /><ref name="n" /> The body of the hare was restored to its original state, and after its recovery, revealed its true form as a god.<ref name="herbert" /> In gratitude, the hare told Ōnamuchi, the lowest born in the family, that he would marry Princess Yakami.<ref name="bierlein" />
The Hare of Inaba legend emphasizes the benevolence of Ōnamuchi, who was later enshrined at the [[Izumo-taisha]].<ref name="n" /><ref name="picken" /> Japanese scholars have traditionally interpreted the struggle between the kind Ōnamuchi and his wrathful eighty brothers as a symbolic representation of civilization and barbarism in the emergent Japanese state.<ref name="bierlein" />
The version of the Hare of Inaba legend told in the ''Kojiki'' has been compared to similar myths from {{ill|Java|en|Java|qid=Q3757|lt=Java|1=Java}} in {{ill|Indonesia|en|Indonesia|qid=Q252|lt=Indonesia|1=Indonesia}}, {{ill|Sri Lanka|en|Sri Lanka|qid=Q854|lt=Sri Lanka|1=Sri Lanka}}, and {{ill|India|en|India|qid=Q668|lt=India|1=India}}.<ref name="n" /><ref name="kokushi" />
==Ise ga Naru version==
Long ago, when Japanese goddess [[Amaterasu]] and her entourage traveled around at the boundary of Yakami in Inaba, they were looking for a place for their temporary palace, suddenly a white hare appeared. The white hare bit Amaterasu's clothes and took her to an appropriate place for a temporary palace along Nakayama mountain and Reiseki mountain. About two hours' walk, accompanied by the white hare, Amaterasu reached a mountain top plain, which is now called Ise ga naru. Then, the white hare disappeared at Ise ga naru.<ref name="yazu" />
The place of this legend is in Yazu town and [[:en:Tottori, Tottori|Tottori]] city, in {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|qid=Q133935|lt=Tottori Prefecture|1=Tottori Prefecture}} (ancient Inaba and Houki), where the shrine [[:en:Hakuto Jinja (Tottori)|Hakuto Jinja]] reveres the white hare.<ref name="herbert" />
==Footnotes==
{{notelist}}
==References== {{reflist|2|refs=
<ref name="bierlein">{{cite book | last = Bierlein | first = J.F. | title = Living myths: how myth gives meaning to human experience | publisher = Ballantine Pub. Group | location = New York | year = 2010 | isbn = 9780307434388}}</ref>
<ref name="d">{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = 因幡の白兎 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-12-14 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | trans-title = Hare of Inaba | oclc = 56431036 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 | url-status = dead }}</ref>
<ref name="hakuto">{{cite web | url = http://yokoso.pref.tottori.jp/dd.aspx?menuid=1069 | script-title=ja:白兎海岸 |trans-title=Hakoto Coast | publisher = Tottori Tourist Organization | location = Tottori, Tottori Prefecture, Japan | access-date = Jan 26, 2013 | language = ja}}</ref>
<ref name="herbert">{{cite book | last = Herbert | first = Jean | title = Shinto: at the fountainhead of Japan | publisher = Routledge | location = London | year = 2011 | isbn = 9780203842164 | page = 281}}</ref>
<ref name="keta">{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Kokugo Daijiten | title = 気多前 | trans-title = Keta no Saki | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-02-01 | year = 2013 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | oclc = 56431036 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 | url-status = dead }}</ref>
<ref name="kokugo">{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Kokugo Daijiten | title = 因幡の白兎 | trans-title = Hare of Inaba | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2013-01-26 | year = 2013 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | oclc = 56431036 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 | url-status = dead }}</ref>
<ref name="kokushi">{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Kokushi Daijiten | title = 因幡の白兎 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2013-01-26 | year = 2013 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | trans-title = Hare of Inaba | oclc = 683276033 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 | url-status = dead }}</ref>
<ref name="merriam">{{cite book | editor-last = Doniger | editor-first = Doniger | title = Merriam-Webster's encyclopedia of world religions | publisher = Merriam-Webster | location = Springfield, Mass. | year = 1999 | isbn = 9780877790440 | page = [https://archive.org/details/isbn_9780877790440/page/824 824] | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/isbn_9780877790440/page/824 }}</ref>
<ref name="n">{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = 因幡の白兎 | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2013-01-26 | year = 2013 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | trans-title = Hare of Inaba | oclc = 153301537 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 | url-status = dead }}</ref>
<ref name="nousagi">{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = ノウサギ | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2013-01-14 | year = 2013 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | trans-title = Japanese hare | oclc = 153301537 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 | url-status = dead }}</ref>
<ref name="nousagi-d">{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru Daijisen | title = のうさぎ | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2013-02-01 | year = 2013 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | trans-title = Japanese hare | oclc = 56431036 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 | url-status = dead }}</ref>
<ref name="picken">{{cite book | last = Picken | first = Stuart D. B. | title = Historical dictionary of Shinto | publisher = Scarecrow Press | location = Lanham, Md | year = 2002 | isbn = 9780810840164 |page= 228}}</ref>
<ref name="wanizame-k">{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Kokugo Daijiten | title = 鰐鮫 | trans-title = Wanizame | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-02-01 | year = 2013 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | oclc = 56431036 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 | url-status = dead }}</ref>
<ref name="wanizame-n">{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) | title = ワニザメ | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2013-01-14 | year = 2013 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo | language = ja | trans-title = Wanizame | oclc = 153301537 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | archive-date = 2007-08-25 | url-status = dead }}</ref>
<ref name="yazu">{{cite web | url = http://www.tottori-guide.jp/806/7734.html#member01 | script-title = ja:神話「因幡の白うさぎ」探訪 | trans-title = Searching for the "Hare of Inaba" Legend | year = 2011 | publisher = Tottori Prefecture Visitor's Bureau | location = Tottori, Tottori Prefecture, Japan | access-date = Jan 26, 2013 | language = ja | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20130307134017/http://www.tottori-guide.jp/806/7734.html#member01 | archive-date = March 7, 2013 | url-status = dead }}</ref>
}}
==Further reading== * Antoni, Klaus (2015). "[https://www.compmyth.org/journal/index.php/cm/article/view/13 On the Religious Meaning of a Japanese Myth: The White Hare of Inaba] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220205134626/https://www.compmyth.org/journal/index.php/cm/article/view/13 |date=2022-02-05 }}". In: ''Comparative Mythology'' 1/1, 61-72. {{ISSN|2409-9899}}.
==External links== * {{Wikisourcelang-inline|en|The Japanese Fairy Book/The White Hare And The Crocodiles|The White Hare And The Crocodiles}} * [http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj028.htm Full text of Basil Hall Chamberlain's translation of "The White Hare of Inaba"]
